text: The video shows a left inguinal hernia in a young man, visible in the distance on the left side of the surgical field.
The surgeon begins developing the preperitoneal plane, revealing the thin peritoneum and the transverse salus, with the rectus muscle visible in the background.
The surgeon begins medial dissection, progressing towards the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament, which are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon navigates the preperitoneal plane laterally and the pre-transverse salus or retro-rectus plane medially. The transverse salus fascia is visible attached to the semilunar line, which is being demonstrated to a medical student. The fascia is taken down below the arcuate line to re-enter the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon sweeps the chord structures laterally and decides to abandon the hernia sac that extends towards the scrotum, minimizing dissection within the inguinal canal.

The surgeon approaches the end of the dissection, ensuring clearance of the chord structures before amputating the hernia sac at the current level. The surgical field is prepared for parietalization to accommodate the inferior portion of the mesh.
The surgeon exposes the obturator space, preparing to position the mesh slightly across the midline. The surgical field is checked for a cord lipoma, providing a critical view of the area.
The surgeon places the mesh into the prepared pocket and begins to unfold it serially. The flap is then closed, ensuring closure of the hole created by the amputation of the hernia sac.
These cases are taking me about forty-five minutes skin to skin. There was nothing particularly challenging about this case. It certainly can be done faster with a laparoscopic TEP.

I think in a young healthy individual the benefits of a speedy surgery are negligible and surgeon comfort and preference has validity. I'm a fan of the TAP approach, and I'm a fan of the robotic platform, just due to the dexterity and comfortability of the procedure. That being said, I'm very comfortable with all forms of laparoscopic, TEP, TAP, ETEP, and robotic, and will adjust based upon what the case calls for.
The surgeon closes the defect in the sac by suspending it to the peritoneal closure, ensuring the sac is securely attached to the surrounding tissue.
And that concludes the procedure. I hope you enjoyed.
